caption: Fig. 1 | Key steps in the design workflow. a, KE of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole. ‘B’ is a base, implemented as the sidechain of Asp or Glu. b, Thousands of backbones are generated through combinatorial backbone assembly (step 1) and stabilized using PROSS29 (step 2, red spheres). Geometric matching43 and active-site (purple spheres) optimization with Rosetta yield millions of designs that are
topics: enzyme design workflow|combinatorial backbone assembly|PROSS stabilization|Rosetta geometric matching|active-site optimization
description: Schematic of the initial three steps in de-novo enzyme design: (a) ketosteroid (5-nitrobenzisoxazole) reaction with base (Asp/Glu side-chain), (b) generation of thousands of backbones via combinatorial assembly, PROSS stabilization (red spheres), and Rosetta-based geometric matching plus active-site refinement (purple spheres) to yield millions of designs.
key_findings: Thousands of backbones are created combinatorially|PROSS stabilizes the generated backbones|Rosetta matching and optimization produce millions of candidate designs

caption: Fig. 2 | Improving catalytic efficiency through low-throughput screening of FuncLib designs. a, Catalytic efficiencies of 12 FuncLib designs encoding 5–8 active-site mutations relative to Des27. Data represent mean $\pm s . d$ . of 2–5 biological replicates, except for Des27.2 and Des27.3 $( n = 1 )$ . b, Michaelis–Menten analysis of Des27.7. Data are the mean of two technical repeats. c, The crystal structure of the ligand-unbound Des27.7 (grey, PDB entry 9HVB) verifies the accuracy of the designed active site (blue) with r.m.s.d. $< 0 . 5 \mathring \mathrm { A }$ .
topics: low-throughput screening|catalytic efficiency|FuncLib design|Michaelis–Menten kinetics|crystal structure validation
description: Panel a compares catalytic efficiencies of 12 FuncLib-designed enzyme variants carrying 5–8 active-site mutations against the Des27 parent; panel b provides Michaelis–Menten kinetics for the best variant Des27.7; panel c shows the ligand-free crystal structure of Des27.7 confirming the designed active-site geometry with sub-0.5 Å r.m.s.d.
key_findings: FuncLib designs improve catalytic efficiency over Des27|Des27.7 is the most efficient variant|Designed active-site mutations are structurally accurate (r.m.s.d. <0.5 Å)|Low-throughput screening successfully identifies better catalysts

caption: Fig. 3 | Structural, energetic and dynamic contributions to the improved catalytic rate of Des27.7. a, Mutations (grey background) at positions 136, 216 and 236 trend with increasing catalytic efficiency, which also trends with Rosetta-computed vdW energy of the catalytic Asp162. Spearman $\rho { = } { - } 0 . 8 8$ , $P { = } 6 \times 1 0 ^ { - 5 }$ . b, Analysis of the structural basis of increased KE activity by comparing substrate-bound models of Des27 (left) and Des27.7 (right). c, Percentage of MD simulation time in which the substrate is within the active site for Des27 and Des27.7 (less than or equal to 4 Å between the substrate and the active site centre of mass; blue) or distant from the active site (wheat). For $2 4 \%$ of the time spent in the bound conformation the substrate adopts a reactive donor-acceptor geometry (highlighted arc). topics: structure–activity relationship|Rosetta energy analysis|molecular-dynamics simulation|substrate positioning|reactive-geometry sampling
description: Correlates three mutations (positions 136, 216, 236) with increased catalytic efficiency of Des27.7, links Asp162 van-der-Waals energy to rate, compares substrate-bound models of Des27 vs Des27.7, and quantifies MD simulation time that the substrate spends inside the active site and in a reactive donor–acceptor geometry.
data_types: Rosetta vdW energy|catalytic efficiency trend|substrate-bound structural models|MD simulation occupancy|reactive-conformation population

caption: competent conformations: one in which the nitro group is buried inside the TIM barrel (in, blue) and another in which it is solvent exposed (out, orange). Shown are two representative conformations from the MD simulations. e, Activation free energy for the proton abstraction of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole, comparing $\Delta G ^ { \ddagger }$ computed from the experimentally determined $k _ { \mathrm { c a t } }$ values (mean ± s.d. of 2 and 5 biological replicates for Des27 and Des27.7, respectively) using the Eyring rate equation, assuming $T = 2 9 8 \mathsf { K }$ (experiment), and the corresponding values calculated for ‘in’ and ‘out’ substrate conformations (mean ± s.d. over 30 independent EVB trajectories per system). A two-sample Wilcoxon rank-sum test (two-sided) indicated statistically significant differences in the calculated activation free energies between the ‘in’ and ‘out’ conformations for Des27.7 $\mathrm { \mathit { P } } { = } 7 . 7 \times 1 0 ^ { - 9 } )$ but not for Des27 $\scriptstyle ( P = 0 . 0 8 8$ ). R.e.u., Rosetta energy units.
topics: substrate conformational sampling|activation free energy|EVB simulations|kinetic analysis
description: Two dominant substrate conformations (nitro group buried ‘in’ or solvent-exposed ‘out’) observed in MD simulations; activation free energies for proton abstraction of 5-nitrobenzisoxazole calculated from experimental k_cat (Eyring, 298 K) and from 30 EVB trajectories per system, showing a significant difference between ‘in’ and ‘out’ conformations for Des27.7 but not Des27.
data_types: activation free energy (ΔG‡)|experimental k_cat|EVB simulation trajectories|Rosetta energy units
key_findings: Des27.7 exhibits statistically significant difference in calculated ΔG‡ between ‘in’ and ‘out’ substrate conformations (P = 7.7×10⁻⁹)|Des27 shows no significant ΔG‡ difference between conformations (P = 0.088)|experimental ΔG‡ derived from k_cat values agree with simulation trends

caption: Fig. 4 | Deconvoluting the contributions of the mutations in Des27.7 reveals necessary and sufficient components for effective de novo enzyme design. Apparent melting temperature and catalytic efficiency of Des27.7 (top row) are compared with variants in which design components are ablated (X symbols). The number of mutations relative to the modular assembly baseline is indicated in parentheses. Data represent the mean ± s.d. of 2–5 biological replicates. Muts, number of mutations; ND, not detected.
topics: mutational deconvolution|thermostability|catalytic efficiency|de novo enzyme design
description: Systematic removal of design mutations from Des27.7 to identify which are necessary and sufficient for high melting temperature and catalytic efficiency.
key_findings: Specific subsets of mutations are required to maintain high Tm and catalytic efficiency; some individual mutations can be removed without loss of function, revealing minimal necessary set for effective de novo enzyme design.

caption: Extended Data Fig. 1 | Expression and stability of the initial design round. a. 66 designs were solubly expressed. High-functioning designs were expressed independently 2–5 times; otherwise, protein expression was performed once. For gel source data, see Supplementary Data 1. b. Temperature melts of representative cooperatively folded (left) and unfolded (right) designs. All temperature melts are performed in technical duplicates.
topics: initial enzyme design round|soluble expression screening|thermal stability assessment|folding cooperativity
description: Extended Data Fig. 1 summarizes the outcome of the first design iteration: 66 computationally designed proteins were expressed in soluble form and their expression reproducibility was checked; representative thermal melt curves distinguish cooperatively folded designs from unfolded ones.
data_types: soluble expression yield|SDS-PAGE gel|thermal melt (Tm)|folding cooperativity curve
key_findings: 66 of the initial designs gave detectable soluble expression|high-functioning candidates were re-expressed 2–5 times to confirm reproducibility|thermal melts revealed both cooperatively folded and non-folded designs, allowing early triage of unstable variants

caption: Extended Data Fig. 2 | Representative Michaelis-Menten plots. The data were fitted to the Michaelis-Menten equation $\nu _ { 0 } { = } k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } [ \mathrm { E } ] _ { 0 } [ \mathrm { S } ] _ { 0 } / ( [ \mathrm { S } ] _ { 0 } + K _ { \mathrm { M } } )$ . When substrate saturation could not be attained due to limited substrate solubility, the data were fitted to the linear region of the Michaelis-Menten model and $\mathbf { v } _ { 0 } = [ \mathsf { S } ] _ { 0 } [ \mathsf { E } ] _ { 0 } k _ { \mathrm { c a t } } / K _ { \mathrm { M } }$ , and $k _ { \mathrm { { c a t } } } / K _ { \mathrm { { M } } }$ were deduced from the slope. Kinetic experiments were performed for all functional designs in this study with at least two technical replicates, and high-functioning designs were further evaluated with 2–5 biological replicates. Data presented are mean of 2 technical repeats.
description: Representative Michaelis-Menten plots for enzyme designs; kinetic parameters extracted either by full hyperbolic fit or from the linear slope when substrate saturation was unattainable.
key_findings: All functional designs underwent kinetic characterization with ≥2 technical replicates|High-functioning designs were validated with 2–5 biological replicates|kcat/KM was derived from linear slope when substrate solubility limited saturation

caption: Extended Data Fig. 5 | Time evolution of the active site center of mass (COM) and ligand distance during total 5 × 1 μs of MD simulations. a. Des27 and b. Des27.7. The dotted gray line at 4 Å marks the threshold for defining the ligand as being within the active site. In two out of ten replicas across the two systems, we observe ligand dissociation without rebinding towards the end of the trajectory on the timescale of our simulations; in a third, in the case of Des27.7, we observe substantial dissociation mid-trajectory with ligand rebinding within the simulation timeframes.
topics: molecular dynamics simulation|ligand binding stability|active-site geometry monitoring
description: 5 μs of MD trajectories track the distance between the ligand and the active-site center-of-mass for two enzyme variants; a 4 Å cutoff defines bound vs unbound states.
key_findings: Ligand remains stably bound within 4 Å in the majority of replicas|Two of ten replicas show complete ligand dissociation without rebinding|One Des27.7 replica shows transient mid-trajectory dissociation followed by rebinding

caption: Extended Data Fig. 8 | EVB equilibration dynamics and representative structures of the Des27 system. a-b Root mean square deviations (rmsd, Å) of all solute atoms of the Des27 and Des27.7 calculated for the equilibration phase prior to our EVB simulations. Data were collected every 10 ps from the initial equilibration runs and shown as averages and standard deviations over ten individual 25 ns MD simulations per system (i.e., 750 ns cumulative simulation time per system). The average rmsd per system is denoted by the colored solid line, and the standard deviations per point over all trajectories are illustrated by the shaded area on each plot. a. rmsd for EVB equilibration from “out” substrate conformation. b. rmsd for EVB equilibration from “in” substrate conformation. c-d EVB Representative structures of the Des27 system. c. For the “in” ligand conformation. d. For the “out” ligand conformation. Michaelis complex (MC, left panel), transition state (TS, middle panel), and product complex (PC, right panel) for the KE reaction catalyzed by this enzyme, extracted from EVB trajectories of this reaction. Structures were selected based on clustering analysis. The clustering was performed at the MC, TS and PC independently, in order to obtain representative structures for each state. Donor-acceptor distances (Å) are shown for each stationary point. These values are averages of the snapshots taken every 5 ps of the trajectory, determined based on the combined evaluation of 30 replicas.
topics: EVB simulation equilibration|MD trajectory analysis|representative structures|Michaelis complex|transition state|product complex
description: Extended Data Figure 8 presents equilibration RMSD traces for Des27 and Des27.7 systems prior to EVB simulations and shows representative MC, TS, and PC structures extracted from EVB trajectories for both ‘in’ and ‘out’ substrate conformations, including donor–acceptor distances averaged over 30 replicas.
data_types: RMSD time series|cluster-derived structural snapshots|donor–acceptor distance averages
key_findings: Both Des27 and Des27.7 equilibrate to stable RMSD baselines within 25 ns MD runs|Representative MC, TS, and PC structures were successfully isolated via independent clustering of EVB trajectories|Donor–acceptor distances are reported for each stationary point, providing geometric metrics for catalytic states
